narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 57% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use to ensure incoming students are likely to succeed. This prediction is based on your profile: you're a Black male from a lower-middle income family, with a full-time study plan ahead, an undergraduate GPA of 2.40, an LSAT score of 34, and low rankings in your school's deciles for Year 1 and Year 3 grades—both in the bottom decile compared to typical students who average around the middle of their classes. While many applicants share similarities like being full-time or male, your situation stands out due to these academic and background factors. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your Year 3 decile ranking, which strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure since it was much lower than average. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also unusually low and reinforcing that risk. Your racial background came third, as it's less common among high bar passers and nudged the prediction further toward failure in your case. Family income followed closely, with lower-middle status contributing similarly compared to higher-earning peers. Your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA were also key players, both below typical levels and adding to the concern about bar readiness. Less influential but still pushing in the same direction were your full-time status and gender, though these align more closely with the majority of students. Overall, your academic metrics—especially those early grade deciles—dominated the reasoning, painting a picture riskier than most applicants whose stronger performances tip the scales toward passing. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to boost your score, build a stronger academic record in any upcoming programs to offset past deciles, and seek targeted bar prep resources tailored for students from similar backgrounds. With dedication, you can shift these odds—many have turned around predictions like this through persistent effort. Keep pursuing your goals; this is just one data point, not your destiny.
